scenario: Nimbus Bank, a retail bank running 9000 loan checks per day in Camunda 8.8, ships a DMN literal expression decision whose id attribute is set to risk-score-v2. Deployment is accepted but the dependent fraud decision can no longer read the upstream result by id.
question: Why does the kebab-case decision ID risk-score-v2 break access from a dependent decision in DMN?

options.text: The hyphen is parsed as the FEEL subtraction operator in expressions, so the dependent decision cannot reference the result by that ID
The hyphen is reserved as the FEEL string-concatenation operator in expressions, so the dependent decision cannot reference the result by that ID
The hyphen is parsed as a path separator in FEEL expressions, so the dependent decision can only reference the substring after the last hyphen
The hyphen is interpreted as a wildcard match in FEEL expressions, so the dependent decision resolves the reference to multiple results

a.text: Correct. Decision IDs may contain only alphanumerics and underscore; kebab-case is disallowed because the hyphen is the FEEL subtraction operator, so the ID cannot be referenced from a dependent decision.

b.text: Incorrect. String concatenation in FEEL uses a different operator; the hyphen is the subtraction operator and that is why kebab-case decision IDs are disallowed.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not treat the hyphen as a path separator; the prohibition exists because the hyphen is the subtraction operator and would conflict with identifier parsing.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no wildcard semantics on hyphen; the hyphen is the subtraction operator, which is why kebab-case decision IDs are not allowed.

explanation: Decision IDs must be alphanumeric or underscore; kebab-case is rejected because the hyphen is the FEEL minus operator, which breaks references from dependent decisions.
